name: /pre-mortem
description: Pre-mortem failure analysis. Imagine "it's 6 months later and this failed — why?" then work backward to find root causes, cascade effects, and specific mitigations. Includes adversarial validation (8-agent multi-perspective analysis), kill criteria for solo dev, AI/LLM failure modes, success theater detection, fix side effects analysis, and operational verification. Use for: failure analysis, risk assessment, project post-mortems, "what could go wrong", stress testing features, identifying second-order effects, cascade analysis, "will this work?", "what's the worst case?", or anytime the user asks about project risks. Auto-integrates with /reflect.

name: /skill-complete
description: Master coordinator for skill creation and improvement workflows. MUST BE USED when the user asks to "create a skill", "build a skill", "write a skill", "develop a skill", or mentions skill creation, skill development, or building new skills from scratch. Use PROACTIVELY when the user asks to "improve a skill", "optimize a skill", "fix a skill", "enhance a skill", or mentions skill improvement, debugging, enhancement, or repair. Consider using when the user asks to "audit a skill", "review a skill", "validate a skill", or seeks skill guidance, quality assessment, or feedback. Coordinates discovery, creation, evaluation (Phase 3a: spec compliance, Phase 3b: code quality, Phase 3c: integration verification), optimization, testing, and distribution. Integrates skill-creator's full evaluation system (evals, benchmarks, variance analysis, description optimization), skill-development's structure guidance, and quality gates via testing-skills, av, av2, similarity, doc-to-skill, sharing-skills, and display formatting templates.

name: /serena
description: **ALWAYS use this skill for semantic code analysis** - finding symbols, searching references, analyzing class hierarchies, refactoring code, or understanding codebase structure. This skill provides AST-based semantic analysis superior to grep/text search. Trigger on ANY code intelligence task: "find all classes", "what references X", "analyze code structure", "search for functions", "understand dependencies", "code navigation", "symbol operations". Use this skill BEFORE reaching for grep/ripgrep - semantic analysis beats text search for code understanding tasks. Even simple "find X in codebase" queries should use this skill for accurate results.
